title: How to Build Multiple Businesses While Homeschooling and Leading with Heart
guestBio: Heather Gerber is a serial entrepreneur and homeschooling mom who founded Guiding Our Days, an educational program supporting public charter independent study students with tutoring and enrichment. She successfully runs multiple businesses while creating meaningful community impact and proving that women don't have to choose between entrepreneurship and family.
excerpt: Serial entrepreneur and homeschooling mom Heather Gerber shares how she built Guiding Our Days while running her tanning business, proving you don't have to choose between family, business, and community impact. This conversation will inspire any woman ready to create a life that fits her values and vision.
metaDescription: Heather Gerber shares how she built multiple businesses while homeschooling, creating community impact through Guiding Our Days educational program.
keyTakeaways: You don't have to choose between building businesses, raising children, and creating community impact.
Alternative education models can provide the flexibility and connection that families desperately need.
Leading with service and heart creates sustainable businesses that truly make a difference.
Building community is just as important as curriculum when it comes to education.
Women can successfully reinvent themselves and start new ventures at any stage of life.
Values-driven businesses create deeper satisfaction than profit-focused ventures alone.
The future of work and education requires more flexible models that support real families.

pullQuotes.text: Women can build multiple businesses, nurture their families, and create meaningful community impact — all without having to choose one path over another.
Community matters as much as curriculum in education.
Leading with service creates businesses that truly transform lives.

timestamps.label: The birth of Guiding Our Days
Advice for women starting something new

content:
What if you didn't have to choose between building successful businesses, being present for your family, and creating meaningful impact in your community? For too long, women have been told they need to pick a lane and stay in it. But Heather Gerber, founder of Guiding Our Days and longtime business owner, is living proof that this limiting belief is nothing more than a myth that's holding us back.

In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus, Heather shares her journey of running multiple businesses while homeschooling her children and creating an educational program that's transforming how families approach learning. Her story isn't just inspiring—it's a roadmap for any woman who's ever felt like she had to shrink her dreams to fit someone else's idea of what's possible.

The Power of Following Your Heart in Business

Heather's journey to founding Guiding Our Days wasn't a straight line, and that's exactly what makes it so relatable. While successfully running her established tanning business, she recognized a gap in the educational landscape that was affecting real families in her community. Instead of ignoring this calling or waiting for someone else to solve the problem, she chose to step into leadership and create the solution herself.

This is what leading with heart looks like in practice. It's not about having all the answers or a perfect business plan from day one. It's about recognizing where you can serve and having the courage to act on that recognition, even when it means taking on more than what others might consider reasonable or practical.

Guiding Our Days represents a new model of education that prioritizes both academic excellence and community connection. By supporting public charter independent study students with tutoring, enrichment, and genuine care, Heather created something that serves not just the children but entire families who were seeking alternatives to traditional educational structures.

Redefining What's Possible for Women in Leadership

One of the most striking aspects of Heather's story is how she refuses to accept the false choice between business success and family devotion. While society often pressures women to choose between being fully present mothers or ambitious entrepreneurs, Heather demonstrates that this is a limiting framework that serves no one.

Running multiple businesses while homeschooling requires exceptional organizational skills, clear priorities, and the ability to integrate rather than separate different aspects of life. Heather's approach shows us that when we build businesses aligned with our values and life circumstances, they can actually enhance rather than compete with our roles as mothers and community leaders.

This integration doesn't mean everything is always in perfect balance. It means creating a life where your various roles and responsibilities support and strengthen each other rather than pulling you in conflicting directions. When your business serves your community and aligns with your family's needs, the traditional work-life balance conversation becomes irrelevant.

Building Community in an Isolated World

Perhaps one of the most important insights from Heather's work with Guiding Our Days is her understanding that community matters as much as curriculum in education. In a world where families are increasingly isolated and children are spending more time with screens than with peers, creating genuine connection becomes revolutionary.

This principle extends far beyond education. Whether you're building a business, leading a team, or creating any kind of program or service, the community aspect often determines long-term success more than technical excellence alone. People don't just want products or services—they want to belong, to feel seen and supported, and to be part of something meaningful.

Heather's approach to building Guiding Our Days prioritizes relationships and genuine care alongside academic achievement. This creates an environment where both children and parents feel supported, which ultimately leads to better outcomes for everyone involved. It's a reminder that in our increasingly digital world, the businesses and leaders who prioritize human connection will be the ones who truly thrive.

Permission to Reinvent Yourself

If you're listening to this conversation and feeling inspired but also overwhelmed by the idea of starting something new, Heather's story offers crucial encouragement. She didn't abandon her existing business to start Guiding Our Days—she built on what she already had while responding to a new calling. This approach reduces risk while allowing for growth and evolution.

Too often, women feel like they need to completely reinvent themselves or start from scratch to pursue new dreams. But Heather's example shows us that we can honor where we've been while still moving toward where we're called to go. Your existing skills, relationships, and experiences aren't obstacles to overcome—they're assets to leverage as you build what's next.

The future of work and education will require more flexible, values-driven models that serve real families and communities. Women like Heather are already creating these solutions, proving that we don't have to wait for permission or for someone else to lead the way. We can identify what our communities need and step up to provide it, creating businesses and programs that serve others while honoring our own values and priorities.

Whether you're considering starting your first business, adding to your existing ventures, or simply looking for encouragement to pursue something that's been tugging at your heart, Heather's story reminds us that it's possible to create a life that doesn't require you to choose between the things that matter most to you. The world needs what you have to offer, and you have permission to offer it in a way that works for your life, your family, and your vision for the future.
